costs: Large-scale institutional reconfiguration of ~200 schools serving 22,000-25,000 students [F-live-education_finance-01]
Transition costs analogous to Poland's 2017 reversal: potential school closures, teacher reallocation, and student placement disruption [F-live-polish_reform-05]
Negotiation costs with church/private maintainers with independent governance [F-live-legal_and_governance-03]
content.equity_impact: Strongly positive if implemented well: removes two of three selection points that concentrate advantaged students, addressing the core mechanism identified as driving Hungary's highest-in-EU segregation [F-live-finnish_reform-02, F-live-finnish_reform-03]. However, transition risks (as in Poland's reversal) could create short-term equity harms for displaced cohorts [F-live-polish_reform-05].
expected_benefits: Reduced between-school segregation and socioeconomic stratification, consistent with Poland's post-reform PISA gains concentrated among previously lower-tracked students [F-live-polish_reform-04]
Improved employment and earnings outcomes observed in comparable delayed-tracking reform (Poland, +3pp employment, +4% earnings) [F-live-international_comparison-04]
Alignment with OECD-average tracking age reduces Hungary's outlier status [F-live-finnish_reform-01]
content.goal: Eliminate selection at ages 10 and 12 by converting 6- and 8-year gimnázium entry points into a single entry point at age 14, aligning Hungary with the OECD-typical tracking age.

implementation_steps.action: Legislate phase-out of 5th- and 7th-grade admission cohorts, grandfathering currently enrolled students through completion
implementation_steps.timeline: Legislative passage within 1 year; phase-out over 6-8 years to allow existing cohorts to graduate

implementation_steps.action: Convert 6/8-year program capacity into 4-year program capacity or expand comprehensive lower-secondary provision
Negotiate separately with church and private maintainers who operate outside direct state governance authority

mechanisms: Removing early entry points closes the three-stage selection funnel at ages 10 and 12, leaving only the age-14 transition [F-live-portuguese_reform-01]
Delaying tracking is empirically associated with reduced between-school variance and inequality in comparable reforms (Poland 1999) [F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-international_comparison-04]
Early tracking's inequality-increasing effect (d=0.44 achievement gap increase) is removed by eliminating the early entry mechanism [F-live-international_comparison-02]
risks: Political conflict: opposition has already reframed even narrower admissions review as 'unified four-year model' imposition reminiscent of communism [F-live-political_feasibility-04]
Implementation disruption comparable to Poland's double-cohort transition problems (thousands without placement) [F-live-polish_reform-05]
Church-maintained schools may resist or seek exemption, replicating prior non-compliance with anti-discrimination provisions [F-live-hungarian_education_system-06]
Teacher shortage and aging workforce may constrain smooth transition capacity [F-live-implementation_planning-04, F-live-school_network_planning-07]
content.title: Delay selection age to 14 (Polish-style single-structure reform)
